content:
# Genesis Chapter 31 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 17v–18v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_031_page_002.jpg`, `GEN_031_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0031 (page 27), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0032 (page 28), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0033 (page 29)

---

## The Great Escape

**1** Jacob began to hear things — word was getting around. Laban's sons were saying, "Jacob has taken everything that belonged to our father. All this wealth he has built up came from what was our father's." **2** And Jacob could see it in Laban's face: the warmth was gone. Laban looked at him the way he had never looked at him before.

**3** Then the LORD said to Jacob, "Go back to the land of your fathers, to the place where you were born. I will be with you."

---

**4** So Jacob sent word to Rachel and Leah to come out to the fields where his flocks were grazing. **5** He said to them, "I can see that your father's attitude toward me has changed — it isn't what it used to be. But the God of my father has been with me. **6** You know yourselves that I've given your father everything I had. I worked for him with all I had. **7** But your father has cheated me and changed my wages ten times over — ten times! And yet God has not let him harm me. **8** When he said, 'The speckled ones will be your wages,' all the flocks produced speckled offspring. When he said, 'The striped ones will be your wages,' all the flocks produced striped offspring. **9** God has stripped your father's livestock away and given it to me.

**10** "Once, during the breeding season, I had a dream. I looked up and saw that the male goats mounting the flock were all striped, speckled, and dappled. **11** And in the dream the angel of God called to me: 'Jacob!' 'Here I am,' I said. **12** And he said, 'Look up and see — all the male goats mounting the flock are striped, speckled, and dappled, because I have seen everything Laban has been doing to you. **13** I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar and made a vow to me. Now get up and leave this land. Go back to the land where you were born.'"

---

**14** Rachel and Leah answered him together. "Is there still any share of our father's estate left for us?" they said. **15** "He has treated us like outsiders. He sold us — he actually sold us — and then spent everything he got for us. **16** All the wealth God has taken from our father already belongs to us and to our children. So do whatever God has told you."

---

**17** So Jacob got up and put his children and his wives on camels. **18** He drove all his livestock ahead of him and loaded up everything he had acquired — all the wealth he had accumulated in Paddan-aram — to go to his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.

**19** Laban had gone to shear his sheep, and while he was away, Rachel stole her father's household gods. **20** Jacob slipped away without telling Laban — he stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, giving him no warning that he was leaving. **21** He fled with everything he had, crossed the Euphrates, and headed for the hill country of Gilead.

---

**22** Three days later, Laban found out that Jacob had run. **23** He took his kinsmen with him and pursued Jacob for seven days, catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead. **24** But God came to Laban the Aramean in a dream that night and warned him: "Be very careful. Don't say anything to Jacob — not good, not bad."

**25** Laban caught up with Jacob. Jacob had pitched his camp in the hill country, and Laban set up camp there too, in the hill country of Gilead.

**26** Laban said to Jacob, "What do you think you're doing? You've deceived me and dragged my daughters away like prisoners of war! **27** Why did you run off in secret like that? Why did you cheat me? You didn't even give me a chance to send you off with celebration — with songs, with tambourines and harps! **28** You didn't let me kiss my grandchildren and my daughters goodbye. That was a foolish thing to do. **29** I have every power to hurt you right now — but last night the God of your father warned me: 'Be very careful. Don't say anything to Jacob — not good, not bad.' **30** I understand you had to go — you were longing so much for your father's house. But why did you steal my gods?"

**31** Jacob answered, "I was afraid. I thought you would take your daughters away from me by force. **32** But as for your gods — whoever has stolen them will not live. Search everything here in front of our kinsmen. Find what belongs to you and take it back." Jacob didn't know that Rachel had taken them.

---

**33** Laban went into Jacob's tent, then into Leah's tent, then into the tent of the two servants — but he found nothing. He went into Rachel's tent last. **34** Rachel had taken the household gods and put them inside her camel's saddlebag and was sitting on top of them. Laban searched the entire tent and found nothing.

**35** Rachel said to her father, "Don't be angry with me, my lord — I can't get up. I'm having my period." He searched and searched, but he could not find the household gods.

---

**36** Now Jacob was furious. He confronted Laban and let him have it. "What have I done wrong?" Jacob demanded. "What is my crime? What have I done that you've come after me in hot pursuit? **37** You've gone through everything I own — what have you found that belongs to you? Put it here, in front of both our families, and let them judge between us.

**38** "I worked for you twenty years. In all that time, your ewes and your she-goats never miscarried, and I never ate a single ram from your flocks. **39** Whenever an animal was torn apart by wild beasts, I never brought it to you and said, 'Not my responsibility' — I paid for it myself. You held me accountable for every animal stolen by day or by night. **40** I burned in the heat by day and froze in the cold at night, and sleep fled from me. **41** Twenty years I worked in your household — fourteen years for your two daughters, and six years for your flocks — and you changed my wages ten times! **42** If the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, had not been on my side, you would have sent me away with nothing in my hands. But God saw my suffering and the work of my hands, and last night he gave his verdict."

---

**43** Laban answered Jacob, "These daughters are my daughters, these children are my children, and these flocks are my flocks — everything you see is mine! But what can I do today about my own daughters and the children they have borne? **44** Come, let's make a covenant, you and I, and let it stand as a witness between us."

**45** So Jacob took a stone and set it up as a standing pillar. **46** He said to his kinsmen, "Gather stones." So they gathered stones and piled them up into a mound, and they ate a meal there beside the mound.

**47** Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha — but Jacob called it Galeed.

**48** Laban said, "This mound of stones will be a witness between us today" — which is why it was named Galeed, **49** and also Mizpah, because Laban said, "May the LORD keep watch between you and me when we are out of each other's sight. **50** If you mistreat my daughters, or if you take other wives besides my daughters — even though no one else is around, remember: God is the witness between you and me."

**51** Laban said to Jacob, "See this mound, and see this pillar I have set up between us. **52** This mound is the witness and this pillar is the witness that I will not cross this mound to come against you, and you will not cross this mound or this pillar to come against me — with hostile intent. **53** May the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge between us."

And Jacob swore by the Fear of his father Isaac. **54** Then Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain and invited his kinsmen to a meal. They ate together and spent the night on the mountain.

**55** Early the next morning, Laban kissed his grandchildren and his daughters and blessed them. Then he set out and returned home.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The shape of the chapter

Genesis 31 is the chapter where Jacob finally runs — and the reader has been waiting for it since the wedding night when Laban slipped Leah into the tent and Jacob woke up a prisoner of his own bargain. Twenty years of labor, of cheating wages, of outwitting and being outwitted: the escape is both liberation and confrontation. The chapter moves in three acts. First, the private consultation — Jacob calls his wives into the fields and makes his case, and Rachel and Leah, who have their own grievances against their father, give their blessing (vv.1-16). Second, the flight and pursuit — the drama of discovery, the dream-warning to Laban, the stolen household gods hidden under Rachel (vv.17-35). Third, the confrontation and covenant — Jacob's pent-up speech of twenty years of grievance, Laban's face-saving reply, and the strange uneasy peace sealed at Mizpah (vv.36-55). The chapter closes with Laban kissing his daughters goodbye. It is the closest thing to a tender moment Laban has in the whole story.

---

### Verse 1 — Jacob overhears Laban's sons

The Hebrew is vivid: **וַיִּשְׁמַע אֶת-דִּבְרֵי בְנֵי-לָבָן** — "and he heard the words of Laban's sons." Jacob is not confronted; he overhears. The shift has already happened in the social world around him before he reads it in Laban's face (v.2). The phrase **וַיַּרְא יַעֲקֹב אֶת-פְּנֵי לָבָן** — "Jacob saw the face of Laban" — is a remarkable piece of characterization. In the ancient world, the face (*panim*) was the window to a person's inner disposition, their "face toward" another person. To see that Laban's face was no longer directed toward him warmly is to understand that the whole relationship has curdled. The Vaticanus LXX reads **καὶ εἶδεν Ιακωβ τὸ πρόσωπον τοῦ Λαβαν** — "Jacob saw the face of Laban" — exactly preserving the idiom.

---

### Verse 3 — The LORD speaks

This is the first time in Genesis 31 that the divine name (YHWH) appears. Jacob has been rehearsing the situation in his mind (reading faces, listening to gossip) but the actual trigger for the flight is the direct divine word. The promise **אָנֹכִי אֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ** ("I will be with you") echoes the Bethel promise of Genesis 28:15, knitting the two great threshold moments of Jacob's life together. He left Canaan with that promise; now he returns with it reaffirmed.

---

### Verses 4-13 — Jacob's speech to Rachel and Leah

Jacob's speech is a careful piece of advocacy. He is, in effect, asking his wives to betray their father — and he knows it. He begins with the divine mandate, then moves to Laban's wrongdoing, then climaxes with the dream. The dream account is strategically placed at the end: the divine command from the angel at Bethel is the closing argument. Notice that Jacob's retelling of the dream in vv.10-13 reveals information the reader did not have from chapter 30. The rod-breeding episode was not Jacob's own cleverness: God was working through the dream to show him the technique. The rod passage in chapter 30 was narrated from Jacob's human vantage point; here, in retrospect, Jacob reveals that heaven was directing the whole scheme. The narrator thus gives the reader a theologically richer picture through delayed disclosure.

**Verse 13** — **אָנֹכִי הָאֵל בֵּית-אֵל** — "I am the God of Bethel." The definite article on *Bethel* here is unusual — literally "the El of Bethel" or "the God-of-Bethel." This phrase is the divine self-identification tied specifically to the place where Jacob poured oil on the pillar and made his vow (Gen 28:18-22). God is invoking that vow: *I am the one you made a deal with. Now I'm calling it in.*

---

### Verses 14-16 — Rachel and Leah's reply

This is one of the most striking speeches by women in the entire book. Rachel and Leah are not passive. They speak with one voice, and their complaint against their father is devastating: "He has treated us like outsiders. He sold us." The verb **וַיִּמְכְּרֵנוּ** (*wayyimkereinu*, "he sold us") is stark — it is the same root used for Joseph being sold into slavery in Genesis 37. Laban took the bride-price Jacob paid in labor and consumed it himself. Their daughters have no inheritance claim, no share in the household. They have nothing to go back to. So when they say "do whatever God has told you," it is not passive submission — it is active endorsement born of clear-eyed fury at their father. The Vaticanus LXX preserves this: **ὡς ἀλλοτρίαις κέχρηται ἡμῖν** — "he has treated us as foreigners."

---

### Verse 19 — Rachel steals the teraphim

**וַתִּגְנֹב רָחֵל אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים** — "Rachel stole the teraphim." The teraphim (*teraphim*) were small household figurines — idols used for divination or as protective ancestral spirits, representing the household gods of the family. Their significance in this legal context may be more than religious: in some ancient Near Eastern traditions (particularly Nuzi texts), possession of household gods could confer inheritance rights or be a symbol of headship over an estate. If so, Rachel's theft is not mere superstition or mischief — she may be staking a claim on her father's household on behalf of her husband.

The narrator places Rachel's theft in v.19 without comment, then immediately (v.20) uses the same root verb — **וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב אֶת-לֵב לָבָן** ("Jacob stole the heart of Laban") — for Jacob's deception. Father, mother, and son are all thieves in this passage: Laban stole Jacob's wages; Rachel steals the teraphim; Jacob steals Laban's heart. The wordplay is deliberate and ironic. The family that lives by deception is not spared it even in its own midst.

The verb **גָּנַב** (*ganav*, "to steal") will dominate the chapter: it appears again when Laban accuses Jacob (v.26, 27, 30) and again when Jacob says "whoever stole them will die" (v.32) — not knowing it was his own beloved wife.

---

### Verse 20 — Stealing a heart

**וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב אֶת-לֵב לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי** — The idiom "to steal the heart" (*ganav et-lev*) in Hebrew means to deceive someone, to take away their attention or awareness — to trick them by misdirection. The phrase contains a certain gallows humor: Jacob, who has been manipulated and exploited for twenty years, finally pulls off his own sleight of hand. The title **הָאֲרַמִּי** ("the Aramean") is applied to Laban here for the first time in the narrative — it marks the ethnic distance that Jacob is now crossing: he is leaving Aram, leaving Laban's world, returning to Canaan.

---

### Verse 24 — Neither good nor bad

**הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ פֶּן-תְּדַבֵּר עִם-יַעֲקֹב מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע** — "Watch yourself — do not speak with Jacob from good to bad." The idiom "from good to bad" (*mittov 'ad-ra'*) is a merism: speak nothing at all, positive or negative. It is the same phrase used in Genesis 24:50 (Laban himself, ironically, used a version of this formula when agreeing to Rebekah's marriage). God is not forbidding Laban from retrieving his property; he is clipping Laban's rhetorical weapons. Whatever Laban was planning to say — threats, inducements, emotional manipulation — God pre-empts it.

---

### Verse 29 — "I have the power"

**יֶשׁ-לְאֵל יָדִי** — literally "it is in the hand of God (my hand)" or "my hand has power." This is a fascinating phrase: Laban is effectively saying "God has given me the power to hurt you" — invoking divine authorization for his anger, even as God has told him the night before to hold that power in check. The phrase is deliberately ironic: *the very God whose power Laban claims has already constrained him.*

---

### Verse 30 — Laban's gods

**לָמָּה גָנַבְתָּ אֶת-אֱלֹהָי** — "Why did you steal my gods?" The word *elohim* here refers to the teraphim household idols. Laban calls them "my gods." The text does not editorialize — but the contrast with Jacob's God, who speaks in dreams and changes the course of history, is left for the reader to feel. Laban's gods can be sat on, hidden under a menstruating woman, and never missed except as property.

---

### Verse 35 — Rachel's stratagem

**כִּי דֶרֶךְ נָשִׁים לִי** — "For the way of women is upon me" — a euphemism for menstruation. Rachel's ruse is elegant and humiliating: she renders herself ritually unapproachable in a culture that treated menstruation as a state of impurity, and uses her own body as a hiding place for the very objects she has stolen. Laban, the powerful patriarch who has just chased his son-in-law across a week's journey, is stopped cold by his daughter's body. He cannot ask her to stand. He cannot touch what she sits on. Rachel, who has been rendered powerless and sold and uprooted, wins this moment entirely on her own terms. The narrator's silence is eloquent.

---

### Verse 36 — Jacob's fury

**וַיִּחַר לְיַעֲקֹב** — "Jacob burned." The verb is the same used for Cain's anger (Gen 4:5), for Esau's (Gen 27:45), for Potiphar's (Gen 39:19). It signals a rage that has been building for twenty years. Jacob's speech in vv.36-42 is the longest uninterrupted speech he has given in the entire narrative to this point. It is the speech of a man who has finally been pushed past the point of calculation into raw honesty. Every grievance comes out: the miscarriages he prevented, the wild animals he paid for himself, the heat and cold he endured, the stolen wages, the twenty years. He ends not with his own righteousness but with God's verdict: *last night God adjudicated my case.*

---

### Verse 42 — The Fear of Isaac

**פַּחַד יִצְחָק** — "the Fear of Isaac" (or "the Dread of Isaac"). This is one of the most unusual divine epithets in the Hebrew Bible — it appears only twice (here and in v.53) and only in the mouth of Jacob. The word **פַּחַד** (*pachad*) normally means fear, terror, dread. As a divine epithet it suggests "the one Isaac feared" or "the Terrifying One whom Isaac worshiped." Some scholars have proposed an alternate derivation linking it to a word for "kinsman" or "thigh" (reading it as "the Kinsman of Isaac"), but the more natural reading is the awesome, holy fear that marked Isaac's encounter with God at the binding of Genesis 22. Isaac, who lay on the altar, knew God in a way that left a permanent residue of holy terror. Jacob, invoking that experience, is naming the God who is powerful enough to be genuinely frightening — not a household god that can be sat on and hidden.

---

### Verses 44-54 — The Mizpah covenant

The Mizpah covenant is deeply ambiguous. On its surface it looks like a peace treaty. But look at what the parties are actually promising: Laban extracts from Jacob a promise not to mistreat his daughters or take additional wives. Jacob offers nothing in return except his oath. And God is positioned as the surveillance agent who will watch between them when they cannot watch each other — not as a guarantor of friendship but as an enforcer keeping two parties who deeply mistrust each other from doing harm.

**Verse 47 — the bilingual name:** Laban calls the stone-heap *Jegar-sahadutha* (Aramaic: "heap of witness") and Jacob calls it *Galeed* (Hebrew: the same meaning). The bilingualism is not decorative — it marks the cultural fault line. These are two men from different language worlds, different religious worlds, sealed into a wary coexistence by the geography of a stone cairn. The heap is real; the peace it commemorates is fragile.

**Verse 49 — Mizpah:** The famous Mizpah blessing — "May the LORD watch between you and me while we are absent from each other" — is frequently quoted today as a prayer for friends who are parted. But in context it is a warning, not a blessing. It means: *I cannot trust you and you cannot trust me, so let God keep watch since neither of us can.* The warmth modern readers bring to the word comes from a later tradition; the original is the austere language of mutual suspicion under divine surveillance.

**Verse 53 — the plural verb:** **יִשְׁפְּטוּ** (*yishpetu*) — "let them judge" — is a plural verb following the grammatically singular "God of Abraham and God of Nahor." This may be a remnant of older polytheistic language (Abraham's God and Nahor's God as distinct deities arbitrating between their respective descendants), or it may be a construction where each God judges his own devotee. Jacob pointedly swears not by "the God of Nahor" but by the **Fear of Isaac** — his own, particular, fearsome God. Even in the covenant, the two men are not worshiping the same God.

---

### Verse 55 — The farewell

**וַיִּשְׁכֵּם לָבָן בַּבֹּקֶר וַיְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנָיו וְלִבְנֹתָיו** — "Laban rose early in the morning and kissed his grandchildren and his daughters." After everything — the fraud, the anger, the confrontation, the covenant of mutual surveillance — Laban kisses his family goodbye. The Hebrew says he "blessed them" (*wayevarekh otam*). Whatever Laban is, he is also a grandfather who loved his grandchildren. The narrator does not resolve the contradiction. He lets it stand: the man who sold his daughters is the man who blesses them before they disappear over the horizon. Then he "went and returned to his place." The word **לִמְקֹמוֹ** ("to his place") is final — he is not coming back. The chapter ends with a horizon, not a reconciliation.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 17v–18v, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 31 begins partway down folio 17v (following the close of Genesis 30) and runs through folio 18r and into folio 18v, concluding at verse 55 before Genesis 32 begins. The parasha division (פ, open) is visible in the manuscript at verse 3 (the divine command to return), at verse 17 (the flight), and at verse 44 (the Mizpah covenant). Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 27–29 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0031, /0032, and /0033). The Vaticanus LXX follows the Hebrew text closely throughout Genesis 31, with notable points: v.1 — **τὸ πρόσωπον τοῦ Λαβαν** ("the face of Laban"), preserving the idiom; v.13 — **ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεὸς ὁ ὀφθείς σοι ἐν τόπῳ θεοῦ** ("I am the God who appeared to you in the place of God"), rendering the Bethel reference; v.15 — **ὡς ἀλλοτρίαις κέχρηται ἡμῖν** ("he has treated us as foreigners"); v.19 — **τὰ εἴδωλα** ("the idols") for teraphim; v.20 — **ἔκλεψεν δὲ Ιακωβ τὴν καρδίαν Λαβαν** ("Jacob stole the heart of Laban"), preserving the idiom; v.29 — **δυνατόν ἐστιν τῇ χειρί μου** ("it is in the power of my hand"); v.42 — **ὁ φόβος Ισαακ** ("the Fear of Isaac"), preserving the unusual epithet; v.49 — **Σκοπιά** ("Watchtower/Lookout"), the Greek rendering of Mizpah; v.53 — the plural verb **κρινάτωσαν** ("let them judge"), matching the Hebrew plural. No DSS fragments of Genesis 31 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
